perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall not, without the prior written approval of the Disclosing Party in each instance or unless otherwise expressly permitted in this Agreement, use for its own benefit, publ ish or otherwise disclose to others, or permit the use by others for their benefit or to the detriment of the Disclosing Party, any of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall endeavor not to, without what it considers reasonable approval of the Disclosing Party, use for its own benefit, publish or disclose to others, any of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall not' with 'shall endeavor not to' weakens the prohibition. Replacing 'prior written approval' with 'what it considers reasonable approval' introduces subjectivity. The phrase 'to the detriment of the Disclosing Party' has been removed, which could be interpreted as allowing use that isn't harmful but still violates the NDA.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 325C.03 prohibits the misappropriation of trade secrets. By making the non-disclosure obligation less stringent, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation, contradicting the statute's intent to protect trade secrets and confidential information. The 'reasonable approval' clause introduces ambiguity that could allow for unauthorized use or disclosure, violating the standard of conduct expected to maintain the secrecy of confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.03 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.03 325C.02 325C.04 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.03 Version List 325C.03 DAMAGES. (a) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (b) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under paragraph (a). History: 1980 c 594 s 5 ; 1987 c 1 s 2 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially contradicting Minnesota Statutes § 325C.03, which aims to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. By replacing 'shall not' with 'shall endeavor not to' and 'prior written approval' with 'what it considers reasonable approval', the changed text creates a loophole that could allow for unauthorized disclosure or use of trade secrets. The original contract was much stricter. The law snippet provided describes damages for misappropriation, suggesting a legal expectation to protect against such actions, and the changes weaken that protection.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall immediately return to the Disclosing Part y all written Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party and any and all records, notes and other written, printed or tangible materials pertaining to such Confidential Information upon receipt of a written request from the Disclosing Party. At the s ame time, the Receiving Party shall delete all copies of such records, notes and materials which are stored on its electronic memory devices.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall, upon reasonable request, return the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information. The Receiving Party will try to delete copies of such records stored on electronic memory devices.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall immediately return' to 'shall, upon reasonable request, return' introduces ambiguity about the timing and conditions of the return. Replacing 'shall delete' with 'will try to delete' makes the deletion of electronic copies non-mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 325C.02 defines misappropriation, which can include continued use or disclosure of trade secrets after the right to do so has ended. By weakening the obligation to return and delete confidential information, the modified text makes it easier for the Receiving Party to misappropriate the information, potentially violating the statute. The ambiguous language around the timing and the deletion obligations creates a loophole where misappropriation could occur without clear liability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.02 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.02 325C.01 325C.03 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.02 Version List 325C.02 INJUNCTIVE RELIEF. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1980 c 594 s 4 ; 1987 c 1 s 1 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity regarding the return of confidential information and makes the deletion of electronic copies non-mandatory, conflicting with the legal obligation to protect trade secrets. The original text's 'shall immediately return' and 'shall delete' create a clear obligation. Minnesota Statutes § 325C.02 allows for injunctive relief for 'actual or threatened misappropriation'. By weakening the obligations to return and delete, the revised contract increases the risk of misappropriation, creating a legal contradiction by potentially hindering the Disclosing Party's ability to seek injunctive relief to protect its trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall carefully restrict access to the Confidential Information to thos e of its officers, directors and employees who clearly need such access in order to participate on behalf of the Receiving Party in the analysis and negotiation of a business relationship or any contract or agreement, or the advisability thereof, with the Disclosing Party and who are bound by written confidentiality agreements that protect third party information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party should consider restricting access to the Confidential Information to its employees who may need such access for business purposes. The Receiving Party will encourage these employees to have confidentiality agreements.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall carefully restrict' with 'should consider restricting' and 'clearly need such access' with 'may need such access' weakens the obligation and introduces subjectivity. Replacing 'are bound by written confidentiality agreements' with 'will encourage these employees to have confidentiality agreements' makes the confidentiality agreements non-mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 325C.03 addresses the misappropriation of trade secrets, which can occur due to insufficient preventative measures to ensure secrecy. The change weakens the safeguards the receiving party has to uphold, as it only encourages confidentiality agreements, increasing the risk of trade secrets being leaked. This directly contradicts the statute's intent to prevent unauthorized disclosure of trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.03 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.03 325C.02 325C.04 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.03 Version List 325C.03 DAMAGES. (a) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (b) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under paragraph (a). History: 1980 c 594 s 5 ; 1987 c 1 s 2 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original obligation regarding the protection of confidential information, creating a contradiction with the intent of Minnesota Statutes § 325C.03. The original text mandates careful restriction of access and written confidentiality agreements, while the changed text suggests considering restriction and encouraging confidentiality agreements. This shift weakens the safeguards, potentially leading to misappropriation of trade secrets, which the statute aims to prevent. Snippet 1 outlines damages for misappropriation, suggesting a legal framework for preventing such occurrences. The changed text makes compliance less enforceable and more discretionary. The law snippet indicates the existence of legal repercussions (damages) for failing to adequately protect trade secrets. The modified contract language reduces the level of protection, increasing the risk of misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: All Co nfidential Information and all intellectual property rights therein shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party and no license or other right to Confidential Information is granted or implied hereby .
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information and intellectual property rights should generally remain the property of the Disclosing Party unless otherwise agreed upon.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall remain' to 'should generally remain' weakens the property rights assertion. Adding 'unless otherwise agreed upon' introduces a broad exception that could be interpreted to undermine the Disclosing Party's ownership.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 325C.02 addresses the misappropriation of trade secrets, which includes the acquisition of a trade secret by improper means. By making the property rights assertion more ambiguous and introducing an exception ('unless otherwise agreed upon'), the modified text opens the door for the Receiving Party to claim rights to the confidential information, potentially leading to the misappropriation of the trade secret and a violation of the statute. The original text clearly establishes the disclosing party's exclusive rights, which this weakens.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.02 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.02 325C.01 325C.03 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.02 Version List 325C.02 INJUNCTIVE RELIEF. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1980 c 594 s 4 ; 1987 c 1 s 1 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The original text firmly states that confidential information remains the property of the Disclosing Party. The changed text weakens this by stating that it 'should generally remain' the property and introduces an exception 'unless otherwise agreed upon.' This weakening and exception create an ambiguity regarding the Disclosing Party's rights, which contradicts the aim of Minnesota Statutes § 325C.02, which seeks to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets by ensuring clear ownership. The law snippet mentions 'injunctive relief' for 'actual or threatened misappropriation,' implying a need to clearly define and protect trade secrets, something the changed text undermines.
